column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Pyrex Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/glass/soda-lime/pyrex-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Pyrex surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in surface contaminants while minimizing bulk Pyrex absorption
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management
Energy density threshold for effective contaminant removal without damaging Pyrex substrate
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal effects
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Recommended number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for industrial cleaning of delicate glasses like Pyrex to avoid thermal cracking; starting at 100W for light contamination, max below damage threshold (~500W for short pulses). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for 1064 nm systems; academic paper on glass cleaning (Opt. Lasers Eng., 2018).

Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning of Pyrex; slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (up to 3000 mm/s) for light; 500 mm/s optimal for balance of efficiency and safety. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser).

Nanosecond regime preferred for Pyrex to ablate contaminants without deep penetration; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to Pyrex's low thermal conductivity (~1 W/mK); picosecond (ps) possible but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research (Appl. Phys. A, 2015) on glass ablation thresholds.

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to ensure pulse overlap without excessive heat accumulation on Pyrex (melting point ~820°C); 50 kHz balances speed and thermal management; higher rates increase efficiency but risk stress. Source: Academic literature (J. Appl. Phys., 2020) on pulsed laser cleaning of borosilicates.

Fluence range for contaminant removal on Pyrex; below damage threshold (~5-10 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate absorption); 1 J/cm² recommended for safe cleaning without substrate ablation. Source: Ablation threshold studies (Opt. Express, 2017) for borosilicate glass.

Beam diameter for precise cleaning of Pyrex surfaces; smaller (100-300 μm) for fine details, larger for area coverage; 300 μm typical to control energy density on reflective/low-conductivity Pyrex. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and SPI Lasers.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on Pyrex without cumulative thermal damage. Source: Industry case studies (Proc. SPIE, 2019) on glass surface preparation.

Ensures uniform coverage in scan patterns; 50% optimal for Pyrex to avoid gaps or overexposure, considering beam Gaussian profile. Source: Process optimization studies (Surf. Coat. Technol., 2021) for laser cleaning applications.
